message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code (3 hardening passes already applied).

Finding G-014: test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm passes even if the SIGTERM handler wiring (#O3) is fully reverted
File: tests/test_observability.py:162
Description: The test asserts BOTH atexit and the SIGTERM handler are wired, but the SIGTERM half is asserted only inside `if "h" in captured:` (tests/test_observability.py:188-190). captured['h'] is populated solely as a side effect of the source calling signal.signal(SIGTERM, _handler) (tracing.py:141). If a developer reverts the SIGTERM block (tracing.py:131-141) keeping only atexit.register (:130), signal.signal is never invoked, captured stays empty, the if-branch is skipped, and the test passes on the atexit assertions alone. The SIGTERM portion of this regression guard is vacuous on the exact revert it is meant to catch — the conditional turns a should-be-required assertion into a no-op when the fix is missing.
PoC: Delete the `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): ... signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)` block (tracing.py:131-141), keep atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush)). The test still passes: atexit handler registered and flush>=1; captured is empty so the SIGTERM assertions are skipped.
Suggested fix: Since pytest runs the body on the main thread, unconditionally assert 'h' in captured and exercise it: captured['h'](signal.SIGTERM, None); assert calls['shutdown'] >= 1. Remove the if-guard so a reverted handler fails the test.

Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described in current code, the PoC is reproducible, and it is a genuine concern (not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design). Be concrete and cite file:line.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

162	def test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm(monkeypatch):
163	    # span-flush on exit (atexit) and on SIGTERM must be wired and call force_flush/shutdown,
164	    # else buffered provenance spans are lost on container stop. No backend needed.
165	    import signal as _signal

167	    calls = {"flush": 0, "shutdown": 0, "atexit": []}

170	        def force_flush(self):
171	            calls["flush"] += 1
172

174	            calls["shutdown"] += 1
175	
176	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.atexit, "register", lambda fn: calls["atexit"].append(fn))
177	    captured = {}

181	    )

184

187	    assert calls["flush"] >= 1

121	def _install_flush_on_exit(provider: Any) -> None:
122	    """Flush buffered spans on clean exit (atexit) and on SIGTERM (#O3).
123	
124	    atexit does not run on ``kill``/container-stop, so a main-thread SIGTERM handler also
125	    flushes+shuts down. Both are guarded — tracing teardown must never raise.
126	    """
127	    flush = getattr(provider, "force_flush", None)
128	    if not callable(flush):
129	        return
130	    atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))
131	    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
132	        try:
133	            prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)
134	
135	            def _handler(signum: int, frame: Any) -> None:
136	                _safe(flush)
137	                _safe(getattr(provider, "shutdown", lambda: None))
138	                if callable(prior):
139	                    prior(signum, frame)
140	
141	            signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)
142	        except (ValueError, OSError):  # pragma: no cover - not on main thread / unsupported
143	            pass
144	
145	
146	def _safe(fn: Any) -> None:
147	    try:
148	        fn()
149	    except Exception:
150	        pass
151	
152	
153	@contextmanager
154	def run_trace_context(run_id: str | None) -> Iterator[None]:
155	    """Stamp ``run_id`` onto every span emitted inside the block (cross-store correlation).
156	
157	    A no-op when ``run_id`` is None or OpenTelemetry is absent.
158	    """
159	    if run_id is None:
160	        yield
161	        return
162	    try:
163	        from opentelemetry import baggage, context
164	    except ImportError:
165	        yield

content.text: The code matches the finding's description. The SIGTERM wiring is at lines 131-141, gated by main-thread check. The test guards the SIGTERM assertion with `if "h" in captured:`. Let me verify the PoC: if I revert the SIGTERM block, does the test still pass? Let me reason about the monkeypatch and run the actual PoC.

# Remove the SIGTERM block (lines 131-143 region): the if threading... block

python3 - <<'EOF'
src = open('src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py').read()
block = '''    atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))
    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
        try:
            prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)

            def _handler(signum: int, frame: Any) -> None:
                _safe(flush)
                _safe(getattr(provider, "shutdown", lambda: None))
                if callable(prior):

EOF

content.text:
Restored cleanly (no diff). The test passes on the real code, and I empirically reproduced the PoC: deleting the SIGTERM block keeps the test green.

Let me confirm the test runs on the main thread (so the `if` guard would be reachable when the fix IS present, making the unconditional-assert fix viable).

input.description: Confirm pytest runs on main thread

content.text:
Pytest runs the test body on the main thread, so when the SIGTERM fix is present, `signal.signal` IS called and `captured['h']` IS populated. The conditional `if "h" in captured:` is therefore the only thing making the assertion vacuous — the guard exists solely to tolerate the absence of the fix, which is exactly the regression it should catch. The suggested fix (unconditional assert + exercising the handler) is viable since the body always runs on the main thread.

All claims in the finding are verified against current code (3 passes applied):
- Test guard at `tests/test_observability.py:188-190` matches exactly.
- `captured['h']` is populated only via `signal.signal` side effect (`tracing.py:141`).
- SIGTERM block is `tracing.py:131-141`; atexit at `:130`.
- PoC reproduced empirically: test passes with the SIGTERM block deleted.

input.reasoning:
VERIFIED against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (note: source path is src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py, not src/hc_assay/... as the finding's prose header implies, but the content matches exactly).

Code facts confirmed:
- tests/test_observability.py:188-190 asserts the SIGTERM half ONLY inside `if "h" in captured:`. captured['h'] is set solely by the monkeypatched signal.signal (line 180), which the source invokes at tracing.py:141.
- tracing.py:130 = atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush)); tracing.py:131-143 = the main-thread-guarded SIGTERM block that calls signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler). The handler calls force_flush + shutdown (#O3).

PoC reproduced empirically: I deleted lines 131-143 (the entire `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): ... signal.signal(...)` block), keeping only atexit.register, then ran `.venv/bin/pytest tests/test_observability.py::test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm` -> 1 passed. With the original code restored (git diff --stat clean) the test also passes. So the test is green BOTH with and without the SIGTERM fix — the SIGTERM regression guard is vacuous on the exact revert it is meant to catch.

Root cause confirmed: signal.signal is never called when the block is reverted, captured stays empty, the if-branch is skipped, and only the atexit assertions (which still hold) run. The conditional turns a should-be-required assertion into a no-op.

Suggested fix is sound: pytest executes the body on the main thread (verified threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread() == True), so when the fix is present captured['h'] is always populated. Removing the if-guard and unconditionally asserting 'h' in captured + exercising captured['h'](SIGTERM, None) + asserting calls['shutdown'] >= 1 would fail on the revert.

Severity: medium. This is a real test-effectiveness defect (a guard test that does not guard the seam it claims to protect — silently lost provenance spans on container SIGTERM would regress undetected), but it is a test-quality/regression-coverage gap, not a runtime defect in shipped behavior. The production SIGTERM flush code itself is currently correct.

content.text: Confirmed G-014 as a true medium-severity finding. The test's SIGTERM regression guard is vacuous on the exact revert it should catch — I empirically reproduced the PoC (test passes with `tracing.py:131-143` deleted) and restored the source cleanly afterward.
